policy_statement: Evaluate options to reduce grid interconnection costs for municipally owned priority sites.
mechanism_description: NYSERDA plans to evaluate ways to lower grid interconnection costs for municipally owned priority sites, helping enable more clean energy projects to connect to the grid and displace fossil electricity generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Grid access rules/costs → ↑ renewable/clean generation interconnections → ↓ fossil electricity generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Lower administrative/technical/financial barriers (especially interconnection fees and related upgrade cost allocation) for connecting clean energy projects at municipal sites to the electric grid. (2) Mediating process: Faster/cheaper interconnection increases the number and capacity of renewable/clean resources that can export electricity; added low/zero-carbon supply changes dispatch and investment, reducing utilization and retirement risk for fossil generators. (3) Outcome: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels for electricity and associated CO₂e emissions (with possible co-benefits of improved system flexibility and reduced curtailment).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased clean electricity deployment leading to displacement of fossil generation and reduced power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: lowering interconnection costs increases renewable/clean project interconnections, which displaces fossil electricity generation and reduces GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while actions may also improve grid efficiency or reliability, the core intended and largest climate effect in this mechanism is emissions mitigation via fossil displacement, not adaptation (hazard/exposure reduction), resource efficiency (same service with less energy/material), or nature-based carbon sinks.  
3) Edge cases applied: if interconnection changes primarily enabled new fossil generation or major load growth without clean supply, the dominant effect could differ, but that is not consistent with the described mechanism (“enable more clean energy projects”).  
4) Applies to ALL instances of this mechanism: any policy whose mechanism is specifically “interconnection cost reduction → renewable integration and fossil displacement” has mitigation as the primary category because the causal chain culminates in reduced power-sector emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., grid modernization, renewable integration, market reform) is deferred to policy-level evidence and is not determined here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge case if reduced interconnection costs predominantly facilitate non-renewable generation, increase net emissions due to induced demand, or if the grid is already fully decarbonized (then marginal mitigation is small). Otherwise straightforward.
key_indicators: “evaluate options”; “reduce interconnection costs”; “grid interconnection costs”; “municipally owned priority sites”; “in collaboration with the clean energy industry”
co_benefits: Potential grid reliability/modernization and reduced project delays/transaction costs, but not explicit enough to classify separately.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could later translate into an incentive-program (rebates/grants) or pricing-mechanism/tariff reform, but the verb “evaluate options” indicates a strategy/planning step rather than an implemented cost-reduction instrument.
